texto: Eudora Welty singles out for praise Austen's "habit of seeing both sides of her own subject - of seeing it indeed in the round". ... Both men and women can be vain about their appearances, selfish about money, overawed by rank, and limited by parochialism; both men and women can function capably, think profoundly, feel deeply, create imaginatively, laugh wittily, and love faithfully. Without vindicating the rights of anyone directly, Austen posits a humanism far ahead of her time. "How really modern she is, after all," Welty concludes of Austen.
explicacao: Citação de Emily Auerbach sobre vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. “Eudora Welty singles out for praise Austen's "habit of seeing both sides of her own subject - of …” articula reflexao, austen, humanism em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como humanism.

Both men and women can be vain about their appearances, selfish about money, overawed by rank, and limited by parochialism; both men and women can function capably, think profoundly, feel deeply, create imaginatively, laugh wittily, and love faithfully. Without vindicating the rights of anyone directly, Austen posits a humanism far ahead of her time. "How really modern she is, after all," Welty concludes of Austen.

texto: Like Wollstonecraft, Austen rejects the notion that ‘man was made to reason, woman to feel.’ Perhaps Austen was tired of reading passages in conduct books suggesting that young women were innately sensitive, quivering, emotional messes.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Emily Auerbach: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a reflexao. O trecho “Like Wollstonecraft, Austen rejects the notion that ‘man was made to reason, woman to feel.’ Perh…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, austen, jane-austen pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de jane-austen e women.
